title: Nigeria's Big Step Toward Africa's Trade Dream
gist: World Bank trade post on Nigeria advancing AfCFTA implementation — adopting a tariff schedule on 80% of goods, ratifying the Digital Trade Protocol, and removing import bans — against a backdrop where intra-African trade is only ~14% of the total. It quantifies AfCFTA's upside (32% export boost by 2035, 50 million out of extreme poverty) and flags the unfinished agenda of non-tariff barriers and digital-trade law.

insights.claim: Intra-African trade is only about 14% of the continent's total, versus roughly 60% within the EU and 50% within Asia.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Quantifies the structural under-integration that AfCFTA targets — the size of the gap is the size of the opportunity.
insights.quote: Intra-African trade sits at around 14% of the continent's total, compared to roughly 60% within the European Union and 50% within Asia.
insights.locator: African countries still trade more with the rest of the world

insights.claim: AfCFTA, covering 54 of 55 African Union member states, is the world's largest free-trade initiative by number of participating countries but has seen slow, uneven implementation.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Implementation lag, not political will, is the binding constraint — the watch item for whether the bloc's potential materializes.
insights.quote: Despite widespread political support, implementation has been slow and uneven.
insights.locator: African countries still trade more with the rest of the world

insights.claim: Nigeria (≈237 million people, ~$290 billion GDP in 2025) adopted a tariff liberalization schedule covering 80% of traded goods in April 2025, becoming one of 24 state parties to publish schedules as of May 2026.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Africa's largest economy operationalizing AfCFTA is a credibility milestone that can pull laggard states forward.
insights.quote: it formally adopted a tariff liberalization schedule covering 80% of traded goods, becoming one of the 24 state parties to have published their schedules as of May 2026

insights.claim: Nigeria ratified the AfCFTA Digital Trade Protocol in November 2025 and adopted measures in April 2026 to remove selected import bans and phase out Import Adjustment Taxes.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Sequenced removal of protectionist measures signals a genuine policy shift in a historically protectionist market.
insights.quote: In November 2025, it ratified the Digital Trade Protocol, another important milestone for regional integration.

insights.claim: Nigeria was appointed Co-Champion of the AfCFTA Digital Trade Protocol alongside Kenya and South Africa in February 2025 and is considering an NDEG Bill to align national law with the protocol.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Digital-trade leadership positions Nigeria's startups and logistics firms to capture cross-border e-commerce gains if the bill passes.
insights.quote: it was appointed Co-Champion of the AfCFTA Digital Trade Protocol alongside Kenya and South Africa
insights.locator: Nigeria is also pushing the digital agenda forward.

insights.claim: If fully implemented, AfCFTA could raise Africa's exports to the rest of the world by 32% by 2035, more than double intra-African exports (led by manufactured goods), and lift an estimated 50 million people out of extreme poverty.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Sizes the prize for investors weighing African manufacturing and intra-continental logistics exposure.
insights.quote: AfCFTA could boost Africa's exports to the rest of the world by 32% by 2035, more than double intra-African exports led by manufactured goods and lift an estimated 50 million people out of extreme poverty

insights.claim: Twenty-six AfCFTA member states have not yet implemented tariff liberalization and four (Libya, Sudan, South Sudan, Eritrea) have not ratified the agreement, while infrastructure gaps and fragmented border systems keep trade costs high.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Non-tariff frictions and uneven ratification are the gating factors on realized gains, tempering the headline upside.
insights.quote: Twenty-six member states have not yet implemented tariff liberalization, and four, Libya, Sudan, South Sudan, and Eritrea, have not ratified the agreement.

insights.claim: Realizing AfCFTA gains requires going beyond tariff cuts to trade facilitation — efficient border management, digitized and interoperable customs, and measures like electronic Bills of Lading enabled by Nigeria's NDEG Bill.
insights.speaker: Aleksandar Stojanov, Samer Matta, Ruchita Manghnani
insights.so_what: Identifies digital customs and logistics infrastructure as the next investable layer of African integration.
insights.quote: the NDEG Bill, if adopted, would enable electronic Bills of Lading, potentially transforming how customs, traders, logistics firms, and banks handle cross-border transactions
insights.locator: Countries must go beyond tariff cuts
